---
title: "Individual Contribution — Cedric Fernolend"
editor: visual
format: pdf
---

## Cedric Fernolend — Individual Contribution Statement

**Primarily responsible for:** repository setup and Git workflow, dataset research
and evaluation, sourcing and integrating the COVID-19 data, the reproducible
`code/` pipeline, the project proposal, the final report structure, and
publication via GitHub Pages.

### Repository setup and tooling

created the repository and connected it to RStudio, establishing the project
structure and version-control workflow we used throughout
([`d4fcc75`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/d4fcc75),
[`a55e718`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/a55e718),
[`8ee3454`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/8ee3454)).
Later in the project I introduced a branch-and-pull-request workflow

### Dataset research and evaluation

set up the dataset-research documents and investigated several candidate
datasets, notes into Quarto files, reorganising the file tree
([`e754d3e`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/e754d3e),
[`05faed6`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/05faed6),
[`956311f`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/956311f),
[`5854271`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/5854271)).

US Supply Chain candidate initial analysis and documentation
([`3204012`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/3204012),
[`85e50e6`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/85e50e6),
[`71d8c88`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/71d8c88)).
([`f562a4c`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/f562a4c)).

### Initial data analysis of the mental-health dataset

carried out the exploratory analysis of the Household Pulse mental-health data
([`08a1b3e`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/08a1b3e),
[`9daf4f4`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/9daf4f4),
[`44c595e`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/44c595e),
[`df51671`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/df51671)).

### Project proposal

wrote and iterated on the project proposal
([`3b015b5`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/3b015b5),
[`fd850d7`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/fd850d7),
[`a501297`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/a501297),
[`06de582`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/06de582)).
When the proposal was updated, I narrowed our scope to two focused research
questions with named outputs and documented the risks of each candidate dataset.

### Sourcing and integrating the COVID-19 data

When we committed to the mental-health direction, the project needed a second
dataset that did not yet exist in the repository. I sourced state-level COVID-19
case data from the New York Times, verified its licence, and built the linkage to
the survey data
([`a8d5e17`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/a8d5e17),
[`1917614`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/1917614)).
This required several non-obvious steps: differencing the NYT *cumulative* counts
into daily new cases, normalising by 2020 Census population to make states
comparable (cases per 100,000), and joining each state's daily cases into the
correct survey window using a **range join** on the window start and end dates

### Repository restructure and reproducible pipeline

I migrated the project to the required course template layout and built the
reproducible pipeline
([`51a4ea9`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/51a4ea9))

### Reproducibility with renv

committed the lockfile so package versions are pinned across
machines
([`d1fb4d3`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/d1fb4d3),
PR #10), including verifying that only the lockfile and activation scripts are
tracked while the package library itself stays out of version control.This was a shared task, because we had issues with git. So I ended up uploading it.

### Report structure and limitations

I restructured the final report so that each research question presents only the
question, method and outputs, with all interpretation moved into a dedicated
section, and wrote the limitations
([`70c03a1`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/70c03a1)).
identifying the Omicron windows as
high-leverage observations, and documenting the suppressed estimates as **not
missing at random**. For RQ2 I added correlation metrics alongside the plots and
showed that the apparent co-movement.

### Publication and presentation

I published the project as a Quarto website via GitHub Pages.
([`9b69040`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/9b69040),
[`3db7dc0`](https://github.com/CedricFernolend/StatProg2-Group-Project/commit/3db7dc0)).

## Reflection

The most useful lesson for me was that the hardest problems in this project were
rarely statistical. Sourcing a second dataset that could actually be *joined* to the
first, matching state names, aligning daily case counts to two-week survey windows,
and normalising by population so large states did not dominate took considerably
more thought than fitting the regression that followed.

I also learned to distrust a result that looks too convenient. The supply-chain
dataset appeared to have a strong risk predictor until we checked and found it was
derived from the outcome itself. Later, the level correlation in RQ2 looked
convincing until differencing showed it was largely a shared trend.

Finally, restructuring the repository mid-project taught me the value of a good workflow, using branches, pull requests and issues.
